did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically Inception V3 and VGG19, for the classification of melanoma skin cancer. It employs deep learning techniques including data augmentation, boundary extraction using regression and XOR operations, and feature extraction/fusion to achieve classification results on the PH2, ISBI 2016, and ISBI 2017 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a new solution for melanoma classification using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). It details a specific methodology involving data augmentation, boundary extraction via regression and XOR operations, and feature fusion. The authors report original results testing their proposed system on PH2, ISBI 2016, and ISBI 2017 datasets, comparing accuracy against state-of-the-art solutions. Although published in a conference proceedings (CITISIA), it contains original research findings rather than being a narrative review.

title: Classification of Melanoma (Skin Cancer) using Convolutional Neural Network
journal: 2020 5th International Conference on Innovative Technologies in Intelligent Systems and Industrial Applications (CITISIA)
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel method for classifying melanoma skin cancer using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to address limitations in precise lesion segmentation. The proposed system integrates data augmentation, boundary extraction via a regression technique and XOR operations to create bounding boxes, and DCNN feature fusion using Inception V3. The model was evaluated on the PH2, ISBI 2016, and ISBI 2017 dermoscopic datasets, comparing recognition and segmentation accuracy against a state-of-the-art baseline. The results demonstrated that the proposed system outperformed the current best solution, achieving higher accuracy rates of 97.8%, 96.7%, and 95.9% on the respective datasets. Although the improvement was modest, the study concludes that reducing the search space through precise bounding box selection enhances feature extraction and classification accuracy.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target class for classification. Present in PH2 (40 samples), ISBI 2016 (300 samples), and ISBI 2017 (517 samples) datasets.

Benign classes used as negative controls/comparators. PH2 dataset includes 80 common nevi and 80 atypical nevi. ISBI 2016 and 2017 datasets group benign lesions under the label 'Benign' (979 and 2233 samples respectively).
General category used to describe the non-melanoma class during boundary extraction and XOR operations to distinguish pixel value ranges.

all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the aim is 'classification of melanoma skin cancer' and 'classification of Melanoma'. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (Melanoma vs. Benign/Non-melanoma) using CNNs (Inception V3, VGG19) and classifiers (NN, SVM). The primary metric reported is 'Recognition accuracy' (e.g., 97.8% on PH2, 96.7% on ISBI 2016, 95.9% on ISBI 2017). While a regression technique is mentioned, it is used solely for boundary extraction (creating a bounding box) to reduce search space, not as the primary prediction task.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dermoscopic dataset used for melanoma classification. It contains images with ground truth annotations.

use.training: Used for training the CNN and evaluating the proposed system's segmentation and recognition accuracy.
use.testing: Used for testing; 10 samples were specifically used for testing analysis, with average accuracy calculated from these results.

datasets_used.name: ISBI 2016
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dermoscopic dataset from the ISBI series, used for melanoma classification.

use.training: Used for training the CNN and evaluating the proposed system's segmentation and recognition accuracy.
use.testing: Used for testing; 10 samples were specifically used for testing analysis, with average accuracy calculated from these results.

datasets_used.name: ISBI 2017
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dermoscopic dataset from the ISBI series, used for melanoma classification.

use.training: Used for training the CNN and evaluating the proposed system's segmentation and recognition accuracy.
use.testing: Used for testing; 10 samples were specifically used for testing analysis, with average accuracy calculated from these results.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It compared the proposed system's accuracy against a state-of-the-art solution using average accuracy metrics on specific datasets without statistical significance testing.

PH2.notes: The proposed system outperformed the state-of-art solution. The state-of-art solution accuracy values (95.05% and 98.78%) are cited from Saba et al. (2019) [14]. The proposed system achieved an approximate 1.2% increase in accuracy compared to the state-of-art solution.

ISBI 2016.notes: The proposed system outperformed the state-of-art solution. The state-of-art solution accuracy values (96.67% and 96.15%) are cited from Saba et al. (2019) [14].

ISBI 2017.notes: The proposed system outperformed the state-of-art solution. The state-of-art solution accuracy values (94.67% and 94.78%) are cited from Saba et al. (2019) [14]. The text mentions a significant increase in rate for ISBI 2017 datasets, with recognition accuracy increasing from 94.78% to 97.12% and segmentation from 94.67% to 98.67% in the analysis section, but Table 1 and Table 3 list the average accuracy for the proposed solution as 95.67% and 96.12% respectively. The table values are used here as they are the explicit comparison data.

architecture_used.main_model: Inception V3 (for feature extraction) and VGG19 (for boundary extraction/training); Classifiers: Neural Network (NN), Support Vector Machine (SVM)
architecture_used.pretraining: Inception V3 and VGG19 were pre-trained CNN models.

architecture_details.feature_layers: Fully Connected (FC) and Average Pooling (AP) layers of Inception V3
architecture_details.feature_selection: Clustering controlled entropy (CcE) technique to remove redundancy and irrelevant features
architecture_details.boundary_extraction: XOR operation combined with a regression technique to create a bounding box

training_details.contrast_enhancement: Fast Local Laplacian Filtering (FILPF) and HSV transformation
training_details.hardware: MacBook pro Early 2017, Intel core i5 2.7 GHz Dual-Core processor, 2gb Intel Iris Graphics, 8GB 1867 MHz DDR3 ram

classification_or_output_head.type: Neural Network (NN) or Support Vector Machine (SVM)
classification_or_output_head.input_to_head: Best extracted features from Inception V3 (via FC and AP layers) fused by Hamming Distance and selected by CcE

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Gabor wavelet-based deep convolutional neural network
preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation via image rotation at 45°, 60°, 135°, and 270°
Contrast enhancement using Fast Local Laplacian Filtering (FILPF)
Color space transformation to Hue, Saturation, and Value (HSV)
Boundary extraction using Exclusive OR (XOR) operation combined with a regression technique to create a bounding box around affected lesion areas
Morphological operations to refine area and pixel information

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Rotation of images at angles of 45°, 60°, 135°, and 270° to increase the number of images and balance between melanoma and non-melanoma classes.
Use of Fast Local Laplacian Filtering (FILPF) and Hue, Saturation, and Value (HSV) transformation to enhance image contrast.

conclusions: The proposed system, which integrates data augmentation, boundary extraction via a regression technique and XOR operations to create bounding boxes, and DCNN feature fusion using Inception V3, outperformed the current state-of-the-art solution in classifying melanoma skin cancer.

The proposed method achieved higher recognition accuracy rates of 97.8% on the PH2 dataset, 96.7% on the ISBI 2016 dataset, and 95.9% on the ISBI 2017 dataset compared to the state-of-the-art baseline.
The improvement in accuracy over the state-of-the-art solution was approximately 1.2%, which the authors describe as modest.
The study concludes that reducing the search space through precise bounding box selection enhances feature extraction and classification accuracy.
The authors acknowledge that the accuracy difference is low and suggest that performance can be further improved by testing other types of CNN networks and classifiers.
important_remarks: The study is a conference paper (CITISIA 2020) with limited scope and potential for publication bias compared to peer-reviewed journal articles.

The proposed system's performance is compared against a 'state-of-the-art' baseline (Saba et al., 2019) rather than a random split of the same data, which may introduce unfair comparison if the baseline was optimized differently.
The improvement in accuracy over the state-of-the-art solution is modest (approximately 1.2%), which the authors themselves acknowledge as 'quite low'.
The paper does not report any statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) to validate the observed performance differences.
Evaluation metrics are based on the average of only 10 selected samples from each dataset, which is a very small test set size and may not represent the full dataset distribution robustly.
The study lacks external validation on independent datasets outside of PH2, ISBI 2016, and ISBI 2017, limiting generalizability to other clinical populations or imaging devices.
The methodology does not address the removal of hairs or veins from the skin, which are known confounding factors in dermoscopic image analysis.
Clinical relevance is limited as the study does not incorporate clinical metadata (e.g., patient age, family history) that are crucial for melanoma diagnosis in practice.

The hardware used for development (MacBook Pro with 8GB RAM and Intel Iris Graphics) is modest for deep learning tasks, potentially limiting the complexity of models that could be practically deployed in resource-constrained settings.
The paper acknowledges that performance could be further improved by testing other CNN architectures and classifiers, suggesting the current solution may not be optimal.
